Elicits hallmark cluster features: severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short attacks (typically 15–180 minutes), circadian/circannual periodicity, ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis), and restlessness/agitation during attacks.

Assesses red flags requiring alternate workup/escalation: new neurologic deficits, thunderclap onset, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression, cancer history, pregnancy/postpartum hypercoagulability concerns, atypical age of onset, or persistent headache between attacks.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: confirms allergies, checks contraindications (notably cardiovascular disease/uncontrolled hypertension for triptans; conduction disease for verapamil), and checks drug interactions (e.g., MAOIs/ergot derivatives/serotonergic combinations; verapamil CYP interactions).

Provides clear dosing/administration counseling for acute therapies and sets expectations for response and when to seek urgent care (e.g., chest pain after triptan, severe dyspnea, neurologic symptoms).

Discusses preventive strategy and monitoring (verapamil titration with ECG monitoring) and mentions transitional options (e.g., short steroid bridge or occipital nerve block) as possibilities to be managed/confirmed with specialist.
Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., opioids as primary therapy; triptans without screening for contraindications; oxygen without proper delivery method).
